channel.title: Psychology Today – Latest
channel.description: Latest articles from Psychology Today (full content)

item.title: When the World Feels Scary, These 2 Questions Can Help
item.description:
Stress

When the World Feels Scary, These 2 Questions Can Help

When we're overwhelmed it can be helpful to focus on what is controllable.

Posted March 23, 2026

Key points
  • Trying to detect and process potential threats can leave us feeling anxious and exhausted.
  • Grounding techniques can be an effective way to manage negative feelings.
  • Focusing on where we are and what we can control can help to increase personal agency and coping skills.

“…grant me the serenity to accept the things I cannot change, the courage to change the things I can, and the wisdom to know the difference.” (Niebhur, 1940)

Some days, the world can feel pretty heavy. Whether it’s talk of war filling the news or worries about the economy, life can feel unpredictable and a bit scary. Constantly trying to detect and process these threats alongside the existing challenges of our day-to-day lives can leave even the most relaxed person feeling anxious, exhausted, and overwhelmed. The good news, though, is that there are some simple things we can do to help bring us back to a sense of clarity and control.

When your brain perceives a threat, it activates a stress response designed to keep you safe and help you plan a response to the threat. However, in modern life, many of the threats we see are neither immediate nor physical. They’re wider, abstract fears, such as global events, political challenges, or things outside our control. Scott et al (2025) found that focusing on fears can negatively impact performance and well-being, particularly when there’s nothing you can actually do to change the situation. The danger, therefore, is that we remain stuck in this cycle, where we perceive danger but have no way to act on it. Fortunately, there is a solution for this incongruity, based on the principles of grounding.

Grounding techniques are a category of therapeutic tools that can be used to help calm people during stress or overwhelm and help to reorient them to the present. In other words, they interrupt the cycle of worry and help them to feel calm and safe in the present moment. Hammond and Brown (2025) explored the application of grounding techniques, such as asking questions to reorient an individual to the present or encouraging them to touch a sensory object during a panic attack. They found that grounding techniques had the ability to calm and distract individuals from the experience of emotion dysregulation and distress.

So how can we use this approach to help with our fears on a day-to-day basis? Here are 2 key questions to ask yourself:

  • What’s actually happening right now? When we start to worry or feel anxious, our mind can race ahead, and we can start to catastrophise or worry about things that haven’t even happened. Explicitly asking this question helps to interrupt this "what if" thinking by anchoring us in the present moment and focusing on observable reality (i.e., what is), rather than what might be. One way to expand on this question is to use the five-senses technique (FST). This requires you to identify five things that you can see, four things you can touch, three you can hear, two you can smell, and one that you can taste. Taking the time to work through this can help to ground you in the present moment, which is (hopefully) a lot less threatening than the fear or threat that you were previously focusing on. This can reduce the intensity of the threat and help your body to settle.
  • What am I in control of? Asking yourself this question is a great way to shift your focus to only what is manageable and within your control. This might be deciding to turn off the news or taking action to get on top of your finances to plan for a possible downturn in the economy. It might even be choosing to eat a healthy meal rather than snack food, or going for a walk rather than scrolling through your phone. Whatever you choose to do, the fact that you’ve made the choice for yourself can help to build your sense of personal agency. In other words, you’re choosing to take control of the things that are within your power and in doing so, you’re focusing less on those things that you can’t control.

When the world feels scary, it’s easy to get overwhelmed. None of us knows what’s around the corner, but having the ability to ground yourself when things feel uncertain can help you focus on the here and now, strengthen your sense of agency, and help you feel ready for whatever comes next. As Wayne Dyer put it, “I cannot always control what goes on outside. But I can always control what goes on inside."

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Gill Harrop Ph.D.

item.title: From Contender to Champion
item.description:
Sport and Competition

From Contender to Champion

How to build a mindset that thrives under pressure.

Posted March 23, 2026

Key points
  • Being a champion isn't just about talent—it’s about staying loose and trusting your training under pressure.
  • Higher stakes shift focus from playing to protecting—and that tightens performance.
  • Champions regulate pressure, drop mistakes fast, and refocus immediately.
Source: Dr. Klara Gubacs-Collins / Used With Permission

In March Madness—where every possession can end a season—the difference between contenders and champions isn’t just talent, it’s who can stay composed and rise when the moment gets heavy.

Anyone who has watched children play sports can see this in action. Young athletes run, throw, and react instinctively. Mistakes don’t derail them—they recover and keep moving. Their focus is on the game itself, not the outcome.

To explore this further, I spoke with Dr. Klara Gubacs-Collins, a lifelong athlete, performance strategist, and former professor who has worked in elite environments. She notes that what separates top performers isn’t the absence of pressure—they feel it too—but they’ve learned to regulate it, stay present, perform under pressure, and execute when it matters most. These lessons aren’t just for elite athletes—they apply to anyone facing high-stakes moments, whether in work, school, or everyday challenges.

The Escalation of Stakes

As individuals take on greater challenges and stakes rise, even small mistakes can trigger stress responses that disrupt focus and execution—a pattern observed in research on stress, anxiety, and performance.

For example, studies with athlete populations show that as expectations and perceived pressure increase, so do stress and anxiety levels, which can interfere with attention and decision-making. For example, research on volleyball players found that higher perceived stress and learning-related anxiety were linked to greater psychological strain. In short, the more our identity is tied to the outcome, the more our nervous system treats mistakes as threats rather than useful information.

For instance, an athlete might play confidently when no one is watching and the stakes feel low. But as recognition grows and the spotlight tightens, suddenly every move carries weight—they’re no longer just chasing a chance, they’re defending their reputation.

This shift affects how top performers operate. According to Dr. Gubacs-Collins, “Instead of focusing on demonstrating skill, many begin trying to protect their status.” She notes that when every action feels consequential, stress tied to identity can interfere with performance, making even simple movements feel tense rather than natural.

Shifting Under Pressure

One of the most significant changes in high performance happens quietly. Early on, performing feels simple: play the game, trust your training, react. But as the stage grows, the moment begins to feel heavier.

Higher stakes bring more attention, greater expectations, and more to lose. In psychology, this can be explained by the attentional control theory, which shows that anxiety can pull focus away from the task and toward worries about judgment, outcomes, or consequences. Under pressure, the brain shifts from focusing on what needs to be done to what might happen, and that shift can disrupt performance.

As Dr. Gubacs-Collins explains, “When the stage grows, identity pressure grows with it. Champions aren’t immune—they’re trained to regulate it.” Even under intense pressure, top performers feel the same weight but stay anchored in the moment, trusting their training to guide their actions when it matters most.

Emotional Regulation as a Competitive Advantage

At elite levels, emotional regulation is a real advantage. Recent research shows that when athletes actively manage their emotions during competition, their performance outcomes improve even under pressure. For example, a 2025 study tracking emotion regulation and performance in shooting athletes found that the way competitors regulated their emotional responses during competition was linked to differences in execution and scores, highlighting how emotional control supports focus and motor performance when stakes are high. It determines whether muscle memory stays accessible under adrenaline, whether breathing steadies, and whether decisions remain clear or turn reactive. High-level performance doesn’t require less emotion—it requires better regulation of it. The goal isn’t to suppress intensity, but to keep it from spilling over.

Dr. Gubacs-Collins indicates that “when mistakes happen, contenders often get pulled backward, replaying the error and carrying it into the next moment. Champions respond differently. They process it quickly, let it go, and return their attention to what’s in front of them.”

Action Steps to Start Performing Like a Champion

Whether you are an elite athlete, a musician, a CEO, or facing any high-pressure situation, performing at your best comes down to mindset and habits you can train and apply consistently. Here are five practical steps to help you perform at your peak:

  1. Separate Identity from Outcome: Recognize that mistakes do not define your worth. Your value exists independently of wins or losses. Pause after errors, label them objectively, and remind yourself, “This moment doesn’t determine who I am.” Reflect regularly on effort and growth, not just results.
  2. Train Emotional Regulation: Regularly and intentionally practice ways to stay calm and focused under pressure. Dr. Gubacs-Collins recommends trying deep breathing, taking a quick pause between plays or decisions, or visualizing your next move. Creating these small routines may help your mind reset when tension builds.
  3. Shift From Proving to Creating: Focus on expressing your skill, not validating status or protecting reputation. Set intentions before performing—for example, tell yourself, “I’m here to show my craft, not defend my worth.” Be mindful to reframe errors as learning opportunities, and celebrate effort and execution, not just outcomes.
  4. Leverage Practice Fully: Use practice to refine skills, then allow performance to flow naturally during high-stakes moments. Break skills into manageable components, simulate pressure during practice, and trust your preparation instead of overthinking during execution.
  5. Redefine Success Continuously: Last, treat performance as growth and expression, not a fixed measure of your identity or value. Try to regularly reflect on what you’ve learned, adjust goals as you improve, and embrace challenges as opportunities to expand your capabilities.

Bottom Line

True champions aren’t defined by talent alone—they rise by mastering their mindset, staying present under pressure, and managing their emotions. When you focus on how you show up, not just the outcome, you unlock your ability to perform at your very best when it matters most. Every moment is an opportunity to grow, adapt, and prove to yourself what you’re capable of.

© 2026 Ryan C. Warner, Ph.D.

Read the original article on Psychology Today →
item.dc:creator: Ryan C. Warner Ph.D.
